message.content:
Differentiator titles and descriptions:
1. Dreamlike Visuality: HIGH participants report brighter colors and foggy, dreamlike vision; LOW participants emphasize reality and minimal visual alteration.
HIGH transcripts repeatedly describe a dreamlike, blurred visual field with heightened brightness (“colors are brighter… foggy vision… feels kind of like dreaming,” “bright colors and a little bit of foggy-ish vision,” “feels like I’m dreaming but I know it’s reality”). In contrast, LOW participants stress that things feel real, deny color changes, and attribute blurring to other drugs like LSD (“Things are quite real… I will remember things,” “I don’t feel colors change,” “blurred vision is more of an acid experience”). This makes vivid, dreamlike perceptual enhancement a clear HIGH-group hallmark.

2. Somatic and Autonomic Activation: HIGH participants notice skin sensitivity, heart rate increases, jaw clenching, tremor, and pupil dilation; LOW participants report mild lightness or tingling without pronounced autonomic signs.
HIGH accounts include detailed bodily effects and interoception: increased heart rate, pleasant tactile sensitivity, jaw tightness/clenching, shaky hands, and requests to confirm dilated pupils (“pleasant sensation on my skin,” “increased heart rate,” “shaky in my hands… tightness in my jaw,” “confirm if pupils are dilated”). LOW reports center on light legs or non-painful tingling from equipment and feeling emotionally calm, with little autonomic arousal. This heightened somatic/autonomic profile distinguishes HIGH participants.

3. Protocol-Analytic Engagement: HIGH participants analyze study design, placebo order effects, environmental confounds, and pharmacology; LOW participants ask practical questions but focus more on personal experience.
HIGH speakers examine methodological issues (“first time placebo… how those survey results,” “environment differs from usual MDMA settings,” “worry sleep will confound faces test,” “oxygen tray risk when sober”) and drug processing (“caffeine helps metabolize… pressed pills have caffeine”). LOW participants sometimes inquire about randomization or placebo but rarely sustain methodological or pharmacokinetic analysis, instead centering on feelings and task experiences. The HIGH group’s meta-analytic discourse is notably stronger.

4. Professional-Boundary Guardedness: HIGH participants feel reserved and mistrustful in the clinical setting, emphasizing staff professionalism over camaraderie; LOW participants feel secure and become less defensive and more open.
HIGH narratives highlight hesitancy and distance (“mistrust… being more hermetic,” “can’t be buddy-buddy… professional dynamic took away from the experience,” “more nerves today in a weird setting”). LOW participants describe becoming less avoidant and defensive, feeling secure, and wanting to connect or hug, with concerns largely resolved by staff support. Sensitivity to professional boundaries and resulting guardedness is more pronounced in the HIGH group.

5. Narrative Specificity and Proper Nouns: HIGH participants offer richly detailed autobiographical stories with names, places, and identities; LOW participants share experiences with fewer specific proper nouns.
HIGH transcripts include named people and settings (ex “Gabby,” “Jenny,” Salvadorian identity, Peace Corps in Sierra Leone, “Bank at the Midway,” therapist preferences), building elaborate, time-anchored narratives. LOW participants share personal stories (Lyft driver, niece, girlfriend), but with less frequent naming and situating in broader social identity or external contexts. The density of proper nouns and contextual detail is higher in the HIGH group.

6. Associative Reframing Cognition: HIGH participants describe making connections between ideas and actively reframing negative emotions into positives; LOW participants emphasize clarity and bandwidth allocation without explicit reframing.
HIGH speakers note “thinking a lot or making connections,” and narrate real-time reappraisal (“got sad… decided to feel it… realized more positive than negative,” “focusing more on positives that came out of that”). LOW participants focus on emotional access vs thinking lanes, compartmentalization, and clarity but rarely detail conscious reframing of negative affect. Associative, reappraisal-oriented cognition is more salient in HIGH transcripts.

7. Task-Performance Vigilance Under Alteration: HIGH participants closely monitor performance, concentration, and memory and worry about confounds; LOW participants report task completion with minimal mental impairment or distraction.
HIGH accounts feature performance self-monitoring (“pushing myself… perform at 100%,” “harder time concentrating… falling asleep,” “worry sleep confounds faces test,” “hyper aware of memory limits,” “pattern detection… pulled out, then back in”). LOW participants often report stable mental focus or toggling between feelings and tasks, missing items only when mind wanders, with less meta-concern about confounds. Performance vigilance and meta-cognitive monitoring differentiates the HIGH group.

8. Context–Stimulation Salience: HIGH participants explicitly contrast the sterile lab with typical MDMA environments and note how this shapes responses; LOW participants acknowledge setting effects but emphasize comfort and security.
HIGH speakers underscore environmental mismatch (“majority of experiences at festivals… surveys reflect lack of stimulation here,” “people aren’t taking MDMA in this environment”), tying context to experience and reporting increased nerves. LOW participants remark that setting can affect experience but predominantly describe feeling secure, cozy, and supported. Sensitivity to and analysis of context-stimulation differences is stronger in HIGH transcripts.

9. Physiological Hypothesis-Testing Mindset: HIGH participants propose mechanisms and interventions (caffeine, electrolytes, glucose) and seek objective corroboration; LOW participants seldom hypothesize physiological tweaks.
HIGH narratives include theorizing about electrolytes/glucose stabilizing effects, caffeine accelerating metabolism, and requests to check pupil dilation, revealing a self-experimentation stance. LOW participants rarely suggest physiological modifications, focusing instead on subjective states and therapeutic implications. This hypothesis-testing orientation is a distinctive HIGH-group trait.

Differentiator variable names:
differentiator_01_name: dreamlike_visuality
differentiator_02_name: somatic_autonomic_activation
differentiator_03_name: protocol_analytic_engagement
differentiator_04_name: professional_boundary_guardedness
differentiator_05_name: narrative_specificity
differentiator_06_name: associative_reframing_cognition
differentiator_07_name: performance_meta_monitoring
differentiator_08_name: context_stimulation_salience
differentiator_09_name: physiological_hypothesis_testing
